content_summary.core_message: The long run is not a test to see if you can run your race pace. Its purpose is to build endurance during the construction phase and maintain endurance during the competitive phase.
key_takeaways: The long run is the longest session of the week and its primary purpose is endurance development.
During the base/construction phase, the long run builds endurance.
During the competitive phase, the long run maintains endurance — it is not a race‑pace test.
Race pace is determined by the combination of training stimuli across intensities, not by long‑run pace.
Missing one long run does NOT ruin marathon preparation.
Training should be calm, intentional, and based on the full training cycle — not on a single session.
training_principles: Long runs build endurance, not race‑pace confidence.
Race pace emerges from the interaction of easy runs, race‑pace work, threshold work, and speed work.
Variation in training intensities accelerates performance development.
One missed long run does not compromise months of preparation.
Long runs can include speed or race‑pace segments, but that is optional and not the core purpose.
common_mistakes: Using the long run to test whether you can run the marathon pace.
Believing that running 32–35 km at race pace guarantees race success.
Panicking after missing a long run due to illness or schedule issues.
Training with minimal variation between easy pace, race pace, and interval pace.
Thinking the long run alone defines race performance.
myths_busted: Myth: The long run is where you prove you can run the marathon pace. Reality: It is for endurance, not testing.
Myth: Missing one long run ruins your marathon. Reality: Months of training matter far more.
Myth: Running long at race pace predicts race performance. Reality: Race pace comes from the full training stimulus.
Myth: Small differences between easy, race, and interval paces are enough. Reality: You need large intensity variation to evolve.

energy_systems: Aerobic endurance development during long, easy efforts.
Aerobic maintenance during competitive phase long runs.
Neuromuscular and metabolic adaptations from varied intensities.
adaptations_targeted: Maintenance of endurance close to race day.
injury_risks: Overdoing long runs at race pace or faster.
Accumulating unnecessary fatigue by treating long runs as tests.
Insufficient recovery after excessively hard long runs.
recovery_requirements: Adequate rest after long runs, especially during peak weeks.
Gradual return to training after illness without panic.
Trust in the cumulative effect of months of training.

training_methods.name: Endurance-Building Long Run
training_methods.description: Long run at easy pace to build endurance during the construction phase.
common_errors: Running too fast and turning it into a race-pace test.
Using the long run to check if you can run the marathon pace.

training_methods.name: Endurance-Maintenance Long Run
training_methods.description: Long run during competitive phase to maintain endurance without excessive fatigue.
benefits: Maintains endurance built earlier in the cycle.
Avoids unnecessary fatigue close to race day.
common_errors: Trying to run the entire long run at race pace.
Panicking after missing a long run.

training_methods.name: Long Run with Speed Variation
training_methods.description: Optional long run with race-pace or faster segments.
when_to_use: As a controlled stimulus, not a test.
common_errors: Running too much of the long run at race pace.

running_form_cues.cue: Mantenha o ritmo confortável no longão.
running_form_cues.purpose: Ensure the long run fulfills its endurance-building purpose.

running_form_cues.cue: Varie as intensidades ao longo da semana.
running_form_cues.purpose: Create the stimulus diversity needed for performance evolution.
running_form_cues.common_mistakes: Keeping easy, race, and interval paces too close together.

workout_examples.name: Endurance-Building Long Run
workout_examples.description: Long run at easy pace to build endurance.

workout_examples.name: Maintenance Long Run
workout_examples.description: Long run to maintain endurance during competitive phase.
progressions: Reduce volume slightly as race approaches.
Avoid turning it into a race-pace test.

workout_examples.name: Long Run with Optional Speed Play
workout_examples.description: Long run with controlled race-pace or threshold segments.
workout_examples.goal: Add variety and stimulate neuromuscular adaptation.
progressions: Increase only if recovery is good.

beginner: Use the long run to build endurance at an easy pace.
Do not worry about race pace inside the long run.
Focus on consistency rather than speed.
intermediate: Use long runs to build and maintain endurance depending on the phase.
Include optional race-pace blocks only when appropriate.
Trust the cumulative effect of training.
advanced: Structure long runs according to training phase: build endurance early, maintain later.
Use speed variation strategically, not emotionally.
Avoid panic if a long run is missed — trust the cycle.

do: Use long runs to build or maintain endurance.
Stay calm if you miss a long run.
dont: Use the long run to test race pace.
Assume one missed long run ruins your race.
Keep all training intensities too close together.
Turn long runs into emotional fitness tests.

motivational_coach_lines: Longão não é teste — é construção.
Seu ritmo de prova nasce do conjunto de estímulos, não de um único treino.
Perdeu um longão? Respira. Meses de treino valem mais que um dia.
Treinar é confiar no processo, não no ego.

encouragement: Boa! Esse longão vai construir sua resistência.
Ótimo — ritmo confortável, como deve ser.
corrections: O longão não é para testar ritmo de prova.
Mantenha o pace confortável — o objetivo aqui é endurance.
Não transforme o longão em um teste emocional.
explanations: O longão constrói endurance na base e mantém endurance na fase competitiva.
Seu ritmo de prova vem da combinação de estímulos, não do longão.
Perder um longão não compromete sua maratona.

deep_explanations: O longão é uma ferramenta fisiológica para desenvolver endurance. Ele não serve para testar se você consegue correr no ritmo da prova. O ritmo de prova emerge do conjunto de estímulos — leve, ritmo de prova, limiar e velocidade — ao longo de semanas e meses.
training_theory: Quanto maior a variação entre leve, ritmo de prova e tiros, maior a evolução. Se todos os ritmos ficam muito próximos, você não cria estímulos suficientes para melhorar.
injury_prevention: Transformar longões em testes gera fadiga excessiva e aumenta risco de lesão. Longões devem ser sustentáveis e alinhados ao objetivo da fase de treino.

fitness_level: Há quanto tempo você faz longões regularmente?
Você costuma definir o objetivo do longão antes de sair?
goals: Qual distância você está treinando agora?
Você já sabe qual é o seu ritmo de prova alvo?
feedback: Você costuma transformar o longão em teste?
Já ficou preocupado por perder um longão?
Como você se sente após seus longões recentes?

structured_timestamps.coach_commentary: Define o longão como o treino mais longo da semana.
Longão serve para construir endurance, não para testar ritmo.
Longão mantém endurance na fase final do ciclo.
Maior variação de estímulos = maior evolução.

structured_timestamps.topic: Perder longão não compromete a prova
structured_timestamps.coach_commentary: Treino é processo, não um único dia.
Treinar deve ser tranquilo, sem neura, confiando no processo.

examples: Treinar é confiar no processo, não em um único treino.
Seu longão está cumprindo o papel certo.
Você está construindo endurance de forma inteligente.

persona.name: Coach Endurance
persona.coaching_philosophy: O longão constrói e mantém endurance — não é teste. Evolução vem da variação de estímulos e da consistência.
